- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# actor-x1
2	
3	An experiment in creating an actor model in rust
4	see [notes/design.md](notes/design.md) for details.
5	
6	This is the main repo of a dual-repo convention for using
7	a bot to help in the development of a coding project. The goal
8	is that this main repo contains the "what", while the partner
9	bot repo contains "why" and "how". The key to the convention
10	is each change is cross-referenced to the other. Thus there
11	is a coherent story of the development of the project across time.
12	
13	The beginnings of that tool is [vc-x1](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1)
14	which currently does achieve this goal, but is being used as a
15	first test bed.
16	
17	## Cloning
18	
19	The Bot session repo is setup as a git submodule which
20	makes it easier to clone both repos. The easiest is to
21	clone both when doing the initial clone:
22	```
23	git clone --recurisve git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
24	```
25	
26	If you forget to use --recurisze you need to do two additional
27	steps "init" and "update":
28	```
29	git clone git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
30	git submodule init
31	git submodule update
32	```
33	
34	And these can be combined using 'update --init' if you like
35	```
36	git clone git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
37	git submodule update --init
38	```
39	
40	## jj Tips for Git Users
41	
42	See [Steve Klabnik](https://github.com/steveklabnik)
43	[Jujutsu-tutorial](https://steveklabnik.github.io/jujutsu-tutorial)
44	and [jj docs](https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/).
45	
46	### Initial Commit for a repo
47	
48	Create create directory add files.
49	
50	Minimal commands to push 
51	
52	```
53	jj git init .
54	jj describe
55	jj git remote add origin git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
56	jj bookmark create main -r @
57	jj bookmark track main --remote=origin
58	jj git push
59	```
60	
61	### Push a change to main
62	
63	Assuming that this is to be push to main you
64	set the bookmark to the appropriate commit and
65	then just push:

68	jj bookmark set main -r @

196	### Why `jj log` shows fewer commits than `gitk`
197	
198	If you're coming from git, jj's log output can be surprising compared to
199	tools like `gitk --all`.
200	
201	jj tracks *changes* (identified by change IDs), not individual git commits.
202	When you rewrite a change (`jj describe`, `jj rebase`, `jj squash`, etc.),
203	jj creates a new git commit and keeps the old one under `refs/jj/keep/*` as
204	undo history. `gitk --all` sees all of these obsolete commits; `jj log` only
205	shows the current version of each change.
206	
207	### Useful commands
208	
209	| Command | Description |
210	|---------|-------------|
211	| `jj log` | Show recent visible commits (default revset) |
212	| `jj log -r ::@` | Show **all** ancestors of the working copy |
213	| `jj log -r 'all()'` | Show all non-hidden commits (needed if you have multiple heads/branches) |
214	| `jj st | Show the status of the Working and Parent commits |
215	| `jj st -r <chid> | Status of the commit, <chid> such as `@`, `@-`, `xyz` |
216	| `jj show | Show the Working commit, -r @ |
217	| `jj show -r <chid> | Show the commit, <chid> such as `@`, `@-`, `xyz` |
218	| `jj evolog -r <chid>` | Show the evolution history of a single change |
219	| `jj op log` | Show operation history (each rewrite operation) |
220	
221	
222	In a single-branch workflow, `jj log -r ::@` and `jj log -r 'all()'` give
223	the same result. Use `all()` when you have multiple branches or heads.
224	
225	## Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers
226	
227	Commits in each repo use [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)
228	to cross-reference their counterpart in the other repo. The `ochid`
229	(Other Change ID) trailer contains a workspace-root-relative path
230	and jj changeID:
231	
232	```
233	ochid: /.claude/xvzvruqo   # points to a .claude repo change
234	ochid: /wtpmottv            # points to an app repo change
235	```
236	
237	Paths always start with `/` (the workspace root, i.e. vc-x1).
238	Each repo has a `.vc-config.toml` that identifies its location
239	within the workspace, so tools can resolve these paths locally.
240	
241	For full details see:
242	- [Git trailer convention](./notes/chores-01.md#git-trailer-convention)
243	  — [ochid (Other Change ID)](./notes/chores-01.md#ochid-other-change-id)
244	  — [ChangeID path syntax](./notes/chores-01.md#changeid-path-syntax)
245	  — [.vc-config.toml](./notes/chores-01.md#vc-configtoml)
246	
247	## Contributing
248	
249	Bot-following workflow, commit conventions, and code style are
250	canonical in [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md):
251	
252	- [Versioning during development](CLAUDE.md#versioning) — `-N`
253	  pre-release suffix convention (single-step vs multi-step).
254	- [Commit message style](CLAUDE.md#commit-message-style).
255	- [Commit-Push-Finalize Flow](CLAUDE.md#commit-push-finalize-flow) —
256	  two-checkpoint per-step discipline.
257	- [Code Conventions](CLAUDE.md#code-conventions) — doc comments on
258	  every file / fn / method, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls,
259	  ask-on-ambiguity, stuck detection.
260	- [Pre-commit checklist](CLAUDE.md#pre-commit-checklist).
261	
262	Task tracking and release details live under [notes/](notes/):
263	near-term tasks in [notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md), per-release
264	details in `notes/chores-*.md`, and notes-specific formatting
265	rules in [notes/README.md](notes/README.md).
266

# actor-x1

An experiment in creating an actor model in rust
see [notes/design.md](notes/design.md) for details.

This is the main repo of a dual-repo convention for using
a bot to help in the development of a coding project. The goal
is that this main repo contains the "what", while the partner
bot repo contains "why" and "how". The key to the convention
is each change is cross-referenced to the other. Thus there
is a coherent story of the development of the project across time.

The beginnings of that tool is [vc-x1](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1)
which currently does achieve this goal, but is being used as a
first test bed.

## Cloning

The Bot session repo is setup as a git submodule which
makes it easier to clone both repos. The easiest is to
clone both when doing the initial clone:
```
git clone --recurisve git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
```

If you forget to use --recurisze you need to do two additional
steps "init" and "update":
```
git clone git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
git submodule init
git submodule update
```

And these can be combined using 'update --init' if you like
```
git clone git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
git submodule update --init
```

## jj Tips for Git Users

See [Steve Klabnik](https://github.com/steveklabnik)
[Jujutsu-tutorial](https://steveklabnik.github.io/jujutsu-tutorial)
and [jj docs](https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/).

### Initial Commit for a repo

Create create directory add files.

Minimal commands to push 

```
jj git init .
jj describe
jj git remote add origin git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
jj bookmark create main -r @
jj bookmark track main --remote=origin
jj git push
```

### Push a change to main

Assuming that this is to be push to main you
set the bookmark to the appropriate commit and
then just push:

```
jj bookmark set main -r @
jj git push
```

Complete example:
```

### Why `jj log` shows fewer commits than `gitk`

If you're coming from git, jj's log output can be surprising compared to
tools like `gitk --all`.

jj tracks *changes* (identified by change IDs), not individual git commits.
When you rewrite a change (`jj describe`, `jj rebase`, `jj squash`, etc.),
jj creates a new git commit and keeps the old one under `refs/jj/keep/*` as
undo history. `gitk --all` sees all of these obsolete commits; `jj log` only
shows the current version of each change.

### Useful commands

| Command | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| `jj log` | Show recent visible commits (default revset) |
| `jj log -r ::@` | Show **all** ancestors of the working copy |
| `jj log -r 'all()'` | Show all non-hidden commits (needed if you have multiple heads/branches) |
| `jj st | Show the status of the Working and Parent commits |
| `jj st -r <chid> | Status of the commit, <chid> such as `@`, `@-`, `xyz` |
| `jj show | Show the Working commit, -r @ |
| `jj show -r <chid> | Show the commit, <chid> such as `@`, `@-`, `xyz` |
| `jj evolog -r <chid>` | Show the evolution history of a single change |
| `jj op log` | Show operation history (each rewrite operation) |


In a single-branch workflow, `jj log -r ::@` and `jj log -r 'all()'` give
the same result. Use `all()` when you have multiple branches or heads.

## Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers

Commits in each repo use [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)
to cross-reference their counterpart in the other repo. The `ochid`
(Other Change ID) trailer contains a workspace-root-relative path
and jj changeID:

```
ochid: /.claude/xvzvruqo   # points to a .claude repo change
ochid: /wtpmottv            # points to an app repo change
```

Paths always start with `/` (the workspace root, i.e. vc-x1).
Each repo has a `.vc-config.toml` that identifies its location
within the workspace, so tools can resolve these paths locally.

For full details see:
- [Git trailer convention](./notes/chores-01.md#git-trailer-convention)
  — [ochid (Other Change ID)](./notes/chores-01.md#ochid-other-change-id)
  — [ChangeID path syntax](./notes/chores-01.md#changeid-path-syntax)
  — [.vc-config.toml](./notes/chores-01.md#vc-configtoml)

## Contributing

Bot-following workflow, commit conventions, and code style are
canonical in [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md):

- [Versioning during development](CLAUDE.md#versioning) — `-N`
  pre-release suffix convention (single-step vs multi-step).
- [Commit message style](CLAUDE.md#commit-message-style).
- [Commit-Push-Finalize Flow](CLAUDE.md#commit-push-finalize-flow) —
  two-checkpoint per-step discipline.
- [Code Conventions](CLAUDE.md#code-conventions) — doc comments on
  every file / fn / method, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls,
  ask-on-ambiguity, stuck detection.
- [Pre-commit checklist](CLAUDE.md#pre-commit-checklist).

Task tracking and release details live under [notes/](notes/):
near-term tasks in [notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md), per-release
details in `notes/chores-*.md`, and notes-specific formatting
rules in [notes/README.md](notes/README.md).

# Notes

See [design.md](design.md)


## jj tips

For users new to jj see [jj-tips.md](jj-tips.md).

```
## Chores format

Filename: "chores-XX.md"
example: chores-01.md

Format of section labels: "## <short description> (X.Y.Z)"
example: "## Topic format description (0.1.0)"

Example chore file:
```
# Chores-01.md
 
General maintenance tasks and considerations for the project see other files for
more specific topics. A chore in a chores file provides quick information on the
how and why of a particular chore.

## Do something (1.3.1)

Describe something
```

## Versioning during development

This is using jujustiu, jj + git and we'll see how it goes. Below is my
git workflow, jj will be different but we'll have to discover that as
we go.

Every plan must start with a version bump. Choose the approach based on scope:

- **Single-step** (recommended for mechanical/focused changes): bump directly to
  `X.Y.Z`, implement in one commit. Simpler history.
- **Multi-step** (for exploratory/large changes): bump to `X.Y.Z-devN`, implement
  across multiple commits, final commit removes `-devN`.

The plan should recommend one approach and get user approval before starting.

For multi-step:
1. Bump version to `X.Y.Z-devN` with a plan and commit as a chore marker
2. Implement in one or more `-devN` commits (bump N as needed)
3. Final commit removes `-devN`, updates todo/chores — this is the "done" marker

The final release commit (without `-devN`) signals completion rather than amending
prior commits. This keeps the git history readable and makes it easy to see which
commits were exploratory vs final.

## Todo format

Todo.md contains two main sections "Todo" and "Done" each item is a
short explanations of a tasks and links to more details using 1 or more
references.

Multiple references must be separated: `[2],[3]` not `[2,3]` or `[2][3]`.
In markdown, `[2,3]` is a single ref key (won't resolve) and `[2][3]`
is parsed as display text `2` with ref key `3` (so `[2]` won't resolve).

Examples:

# Todo

- Add new feature X [details](chores-01.md#feature-x)
- Fix bug Y [1]

# Done

- Fixed issue Z [2],[3]

[1]: chores-01bugs.md#bug-y

# actor-x1

## Overview

Actor-x1 is a system that uses Communicating
Sequential Process (CSP) invented by Tony Hoare
In 1978 and is commonly termed the actor model.

 - There will be a management system (MS) that:

   - Provides the capability to interact with
     the runtime providing control and to capture
     logs and assists in developing the app
   - Creates an hello world scaffolding
   - provides discovery capabilities of other
     other actors.

 - A runtime that drives the system and provides
   resources and services for the system
   - interfaces between the manager and the application
 
 - One or more actors running on one or more
   threads. Each actor adheres to these
   invariants:
   - Has on basic entry point; handler()
   - The handler() handles one message at a time and
     must run to completion as quickly as possible.
   - The handler() never ever blocks
   - If a handler() has tasks that take to much time
     a technique that can be used is to spawn
     threads to do work. That thread would then use
     messages to communicate with its parent and
     other actors as it does its work.

## Future capabilities
   - I would like Actors to be implemented as an HSM.
     This potentially allows an Actor to implement
     sophisticated algorithms and prove correct operation.

## Stage1 runtime

Design and implement the simplest runtime possible

### Message

struct Message {}

### Actor

Actor implements an Actor trait

trait Actor {
  // Handles a message 
  fn handle_message(msg: Message)

  // other methods..
}

### Goal1

- Run two actors on one thread.
- A single message which is an empty struct
- Message ping pongs between the two actors
  for duration in seconds specified as a f64
  in seconds from the command line

## Goal2

- Same as Goal1 but each actor on a separate thread.

## Stage2 runtime

We will design and implement the slightly more capable runtime

It supports 3 actors A, B and C

### Message

struct Message {
  src_id: u32,
  dst_id: u32,
  send_count: u64,
}

### Actor

Actor implements an Actor trait

trait Actor {
  // Create a new actor
  fn new(id: u32, name: &str) -> Self;

  // Return the actors name
  fn name(&Self) -> &str;

  // Handle a message
  fn handle_message(msg: mut Message);

  // other methods..
}

### Goal1

- Run 3 actors on one thread.
- Runtime "sends" 5 messages each to a random destination actor
- Each actor forwards the message they receive to a random
  dst_id that they select.

### Goal2
- Same as Goal1 but each actor on a separate thread.

content.text:
Acquainted. Summary of what I found:

**Project**: `actor-x1` — a Rust experiment in the actor model (CSP, Hoare 1978). Currently design-only; no source code yet.

**Design stages** (notes/design.md):
- **Stage1**: minimal runtime — 2 actors ping-pong a unit `Message`; Goal1 single-thread, Goal2 per-thread.
- **Stage2**: 3 actors (A/B/C), `Message { src_id, dst_id, send_count }`, random forwarding.
- **Future**: actors as HSMs.

**Todo** (notes/todo.md): Initial design, PoC runtime, PoC manager — nothing in progress.

**Repo layout**: dual-repo — app repo at `.`, bot session repo at `.claude/` (git submodule + jj). Cross-linked via `ochid:` commit trailers.

**Conventions I'll follow** (CLAUDE.md):
- Conventional Commits with trailing `(X.Y.Z)`; every plan starts with a version bump (single-step or `X.Y.Z-0`/`-1`/… multi-step).
- Two-checkpoint Commit → Push+Finalize flow per step, with explicit approval at each checkpoint; hard stop after finalize.
- `//!` module doc + `///` on every fn/method; `// OK: …` justification on every non-test `.unwrap*`.
- Ask on ambiguity; don't use the memory dir (CLAUDE.md is canonical).
- "The bot thinks …" prefix for speculation in durable text.
- Last recent commit: `6029a15 docs: Update CLAUDE and README to latest version`.

Ready for direction.

## Stage 1 implementation proposal

**Recommended approach**: multi-step, `0.1.0-0 → 0.1.0-1 → 0.1.0-2 → 0.1.0`. Stage 1 bundles three discrete pieces (bootstrap, Goal1, Goal2), each non-trivial. One-step would work but multi-step gives a clean per-goal checkpoint. Happy to flip to single-step per-goal (`0.1.0`, `0.2.0`) if you'd rather see each Goal as its own "done" marker.

**Scaffolding (0.1.0-0, chore marker, no behavior)**
- `cargo init --lib` at repo root → crate named `actor_x1`.
- `src/lib.rs` — re-exports `runtime` and `actor` modules.
- `src/actor.rs` — `Message`, `Actor` trait.
- `src/runtime.rs` — single-threaded and threaded runtimes.
- `src/bin/goal1.rs`, `src/bin/goal2.rs` — each takes one f64 positional arg (duration seconds). Start with hand-rolled `std::env::args` parsing; add `clap` only if we grow.
- `Cargo.toml` version = `0.1.0-0`.

**Goal1 — single thread, 2 actors (0.1.0-1)**
- `struct Message;` (unit per spec).
- `trait Actor { fn handle_message(&mut self, ctx: &mut Context, msg: Message); }` — **deviates slightly from the design sketch** (adds `&mut self` and a `Context`). See open question below.
- `Context::send(&mut self, dst_id: u32, msg: Message)` pushes onto a runtime queue.
- `Runtime` owns `Vec<Box<dyn Actor>>` and a `VecDeque<(dst_id, Message)>`. Loop: pop → dispatch → repeat until `Instant::now() >= deadline`. Seed with one message from actor 0 → actor 1. Report total messages handled.

**Goal2 — one thread per actor (0.1.0-2)**
- Each actor owns a `mpsc::Receiver<Message>`; every actor holds a `Vec<Sender<Message>>` indexed by `dst_id` (or a `HashMap`).
- Spawn a thread per actor: `while let Ok(msg) = rx.recv() { actor.handle_message(msg); }`.
- Seed: main sends one `Message` into actor 0's channel, sleeps `duration`, then drops all senders. `recv()` errors → threads exit cleanly → `join`.
- No shared mutable state beyond the channels; each actor's state is thread-local.

**Final (0.1.0)** — drop the `-N` suffix, update `notes/todo.md` (move 'PoC runtime' to Done), add `notes/chores-01.md` section "Stage 1 runtime (0.1.0)".

**Open question I want to resolve before coding**

The design's trait signature `fn handle_message(msg: Message)` has no `&self` and no way to emit a reply. With an empty `Message {}`, ping-pong requires *some* way for a handler to trigger the next send. My proposal adds `&mut self` + a `Context` handle (option b). Alternatives:
- (a) `fn handle_message(&mut self, msg: Message) -> Option<(u32, Message)>` — handler returns the next send. Simpler; less extensible.
- (c) Keep the signature literal and drive ping-pong from the runtime loop itself (actors become no-ops) — matches spec but isn't really an actor.

1	//! Hardware tick counter abstraction: thin wrapper over the
2	//! target architecture's fixed-rate monotonic counter.
3	//!
4	//! Probes call three functions; the per-arch impl lives in a
5	//! child module gated by `#[cfg(target_arch = ...)]`:
6	//!
7	//! - [`read_ticks`] — current counter value.
8	//! - [`ticks_per_ns`] — calibrated conversion ratio.
9	//! - [`require_ok`] — exit the process if the counter isn't
10	//!   usable for probe measurements.
11	//!
12	//! Only `x86_64` is implemented today. AArch64 (`CNTVCT_EL0`)
13	//! and RISC-V (`time` CSR) both have architecturally invariant
14	//! counters by ISA spec, so their `require_ok` will be a no-op
15	//! once those impls land.
16	
17	#[cfg(target_arch = "x86_64")]
18	mod x86_64;
19	
20	#[cfg(target_arch = "x86_64")]
21	use x86_64 as imp;
22	
23	#[cfg(not(target_arch = "x86_64"))]
24	compile_error!(
25	    "iiac-perf probes currently only support target_arch = \"x86_64\". \
26	     Add a per-arch impl module (AArch64: CNTVCT_EL0; RISC-V: time CSR) \
27	     and wire it into src/ticks.rs."
28	);
29	
30	/// Read the current tick counter. Monotonic and fixed-rate.
31	#[inline(always)]
32	pub fn read_ticks() -> u64 {
33	    imp::read_ticks()
34	}
35	
36	/// Calibrated conversion ratio: counter ticks per nanosecond.
37	/// Cached — the first call does the work.
38	pub fn ticks_per_ns() -> f64 {
39	    imp::ticks_per_ns()
40	}
41	
42	/// Verify the tick counter is usable for probe measurements;
43	/// exit the process (code 1) with a diagnostic if not. The
44	/// checks performed depend on the target architecture.
45	pub fn require_ok() {
46	    imp::require_ok();
47	}

## Addendum — perf measurement via `tprobe2`

**What `tprobe2` needs** (minimum viable copy from `../iiac-perf/src`):

| iiac-perf file | actor-x1 destination | notes |
|---|---|---|
| `tprobe2.rs` | `src/tprobe2.rs` | unchanged |
| `band_table.rs` | `src/band_table.rs` | rewrite one `use` to point at our fmt module |
| `ticks.rs` | `src/ticks.rs` | unchanged |
| `ticks/x86_64.rs` | `src/ticks/x86_64.rs` | unchanged |
| `fmt_commas` + `fmt_commas_f64` (from `harness.rs` lines 162–188) | `src/fmt.rs` *(new, ~30 lines)* | extract just those two fns; skips harness's 393-line benchmark apparatus we don't need |

**Crate dependencies added to `Cargo.toml`**: `hdrhistogram = "7"`, `minstant = "0.1"`. Nothing else from iiac-perf's chain is required.

**Arch caveat**: tprobe2's tick counter is x86_64-only today — `ticks.rs` has a `compile_error!` on other arches. **The bot thinks** this is fine for us (your dev box is x86_64) but worth naming so it isn't a later surprise.

**Where the probe sites go in Goal1**
- `TProbe2` lives on the single-threaded runtime alongside the message queue.
- Wrap the dispatch call: `let id = probe.start(actor_id as u64); actor.handle_message(&mut ctx, msg); probe.end(id);` — `site_id = actor_id` so per-actor latency shows up separately if we add per-site grouping later.
- After the `Instant::now() >= deadline` loop exits, call `probe.report(false)` — prints the band table in ns alongside the existing message-count summary.
- Add `--ticks` flag (or `-t`) to flip to raw ticks, matching the iiac-perf CLI convention.

**Revised version ladder**
- `0.1.0-0` — bootstrap Cargo + plan marker
- `0.1.0-1` — Goal1 base (ping-pong + duration + count)
- `0.1.0-2` — copy `tprobe2` + wire probe sites into Goal1 (this is the added step)
- `0.1.0-3` — Goal2
- `0.1.0` — final: todo/chores update, drop `-N` suffix

**Notes/chores entry for the copy**: a `## Vendored perf probe (0.1.0-2)` section in `chores-01.md` recording the source path (`../iiac-perf/src`), files copied, and the "promote to shared lib later" intent.

**Things to confirm before I start**
1. Scope granularity: **per-dispatch** (proposed above — one `start`/`end` per `handle_message` call) or **per-round-trip** (probe wraps one ping-pong, actor-pair latency)? Per-dispatch matches tprobe2's design more naturally; round-trip tells you end-to-end messaging cost.
2. Apply probe to **Goal2 too**? `TProbe2` is `Send` but not `Sync`, so each threaded actor would own its own probe and report on shutdown. Straightforward but adds surface area.
3. Module naming: `src/tprobe2.rs` at top level (flat, matches source) vs `src/perf/{tprobe2,band_table,ticks,fmt}.rs` (grouped, easier to extract to a lib later). I lean `src/perf/` for the grouping; happy either way.

"y" to which? I need a few specifics before starting — can you confirm:

1. **Handler signature** (from the first proposal, unresolved) — (a) return `Option<(u32, Message)>`, (b) `&mut self` + `Context::send`, or (c) keep literal spec?
2. **Probe scope** — per-dispatch or per-round-trip?
3. **Probe on Goal2 too?** — y/n
4. **Module layout** — flat (`src/tprobe2.rs`) or grouped (`src/perf/`)?

If you meant "yes to all my recommendations" that would be: **(b), per-dispatch, yes Goal2 too, `src/perf/`** — please confirm.

## Stage 1 runtime — plan marker (0.1.0-0)

Begins the Stage 1 implementation described in [design.md](design.md).
Multi-step ladder (see [Versioning during development](../CLAUDE.md#versioning)):

- `0.1.0-0` — bootstrap Cargo crate + this plan marker (no behavior).
- `0.1.0-1` — Goal1: two actors, single thread, ping-pong an empty
  `Message` for a caller-supplied duration in seconds. Reports
  total messages handled.
- `0.1.0-2` — vendor `tprobe2` (and its transitive deps: `band_table`,
  `ticks`, `fmt_commas*`) from `../iiac-perf/src` into `src/perf/`.
  Wrap per-dispatch `handle_message` calls with `probe.start`/`probe.end`
  using `site_id = actor_id`; print a band-table report at shutdown
  (ns by default, `-t/--ticks` for raw ticks). The bot thinks promoting
  this to a shared crate is worthwhile once Stage 2 is underway, but
  copying in now avoids a cross-repo dependency for the PoC.
- `0.1.0-3` — Goal2: same workload as Goal1 but each actor on its own
  thread, communicating via `std::sync::mpsc` channels. Shutdown by
  dropping senders. Probe instrumented the same way (per-thread
  `TProbe2`, report on join).
- `0.1.0` — final: drop the `-N` suffix, update `notes/todo.md` and
  `notes/README.md`.

### Design decisions recorded here

- **Actor trait signature** deviates from the design sketch by adding
  `&mut self` and a `&mut dyn Context` parameter, where `Context::send(dst_id, msg)`
  is the only way for a handler to emit messages. Required because
  the empty `Message {}` carries no reply-to information and the
  sketched signature `fn handle_message(msg: Message)` has nowhere
  to thread outbound sends through.
- **Two `Context` implementations**: the single-threaded runtime's
  context writes to a `VecDeque`; the multi-threaded runtime's
  context holds `Sender<Message>` per destination. Actors see
  only `&mut dyn Context` and are agnostic to which runtime is
  driving them.
- **x86_64-only for now**: `tprobe2`'s tick counter uses `rdtsc`
  and `iiac-perf/src/ticks.rs` emits a `compile_error!` on other
  arches. Development target is x86_64 so this is acceptable; the
  restriction is documented at the vendored module.

## Goal1: single-thread ping-pong runtime (0.1.0-1)

Implements Goal1 of Stage 1: two actors, one thread, ping-pong an
empty [`Message`] for a caller-supplied duration in seconds, then
report the message count and throughput.

- `src/lib.rs`: `Message` unit struct, `Actor` trait
  (`handle_message(&mut self, &mut dyn Context, Message)`),
  `Context` trait (`send(dst_id: u32, msg: Message)`), and
  `pub mod runtime`.
- `src/runtime.rs`: `SingleThreadRuntime` owns a
  `Vec<Box<dyn Actor>>` and a `VecDeque<(u32, Message)>`; `run_for`
  pops a message, field-split-borrows `actors` and `queue`,
  constructs a private `SingleCtx` wrapping the queue, dispatches,
  counts. Terminates on deadline or queue drain. Unit tests cover
  seed drain, bounded ping-pong count (11 = seed + 5·2), and
  sequential id assignment.
- `src/bin/goal1.rs`: CLI takes one positional `<duration_secs>`
  f64; constructs two `PingPongActor`s (each replies once per
  received message to its peer), seeds one message into actor 0,
  runs, prints `goal1: <count> messages in <secs>s (<M msg/s>)`.

Smoke run on this box: 0.5 s → ~19.3 M messages → ~38.7 M msg/s.

### Deviation from the design sketch

`struct Message {}` in `design.md` is realised as a unit struct
`pub struct Message;` — equivalent externally, instantiates as
`Message` rather than `Message {}`. Matches clippy's preference for
empty types; trivial to convert back if literal match matters.

# Chores-01

Discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible markdown.
There is also a [todo.md](todo.md) file and it tracks tasks and in
general there should be a chore section for each task with the why
and how this task will be completed.

See [Chores format](README.md#chores-format)

## Stage 1 runtime — plan marker (0.1.0-0)

Begins the Stage 1 implementation described in [design.md](design.md).
Multi-step ladder (see [Versioning during development](../CLAUDE.md#versioning)):

- `0.1.0-0` — bootstrap Cargo crate + this plan marker (no behavior).
- `0.1.0-1` — Goal1: two actors, single thread, ping-pong an empty
  `Message` for a caller-supplied duration in seconds. Reports
  total messages handled.
- `0.1.0-2` — vendor `tprobe2` (and its transitive deps: `band_table`,
  `ticks`, `fmt_commas*`) from `../iiac-perf/src` into `src/perf/`.
  Wrap per-dispatch `handle_message` calls with `probe.start`/`probe.end`
  using `site_id = actor_id`; print a band-table report at shutdown
  (ns by default, `-t/--ticks` for raw ticks). The bot thinks promoting
  this to a shared crate is worthwhile once Stage 2 is underway, but
  copying in now avoids a cross-repo dependency for the PoC.
- `0.1.0-3` — Goal2: same workload as Goal1 but each actor on its own
  thread, communicating via `std::sync::mpsc` channels. Shutdown by
  dropping senders. Probe instrumented the same way (per-thread
  `TProbe2`, report on join).
- `0.1.0` — final: drop the `-N` suffix, update `notes/todo.md` and
  `notes/README.md`.

### Design decisions recorded here

- **Actor trait signature** deviates from the design sketch by adding
  `&mut self` and a `&mut dyn Context` parameter, where `Context::send(dst_id, msg)`
  is the only way for a handler to emit messages. Required because
  the empty `Message {}` carries no reply-to information and the
  sketched signature `fn handle_message(msg: Message)` has nowhere
  to thread outbound sends through.
- **Two `Context` implementations**: the single-threaded runtime's
  context writes to a `VecDeque`; the multi-threaded runtime's
  context holds `Sender<Message>` per destination. Actors see
  only `&mut dyn Context` and are agnostic to which runtime is
  driving them.
- **x86_64-only for now**: `tprobe2`'s tick counter uses `rdtsc`
  and `iiac-perf/src/ticks.rs` emits a `compile_error!` on other
  arches. Development target is x86_64 so this is acceptable; the
  restriction is documented at the vendored module.

input.new_string:
### Deviation from the design sketch

`struct Message {}` in `design.md` is realised as a unit struct
`pub struct Message;` — equivalent externally, instantiates as
`Message` rather than `Message {}`. Matches clippy's preference for
empty types; trivial to convert back if literal match matters.

## Vendored perf probe + Goal1 instrumentation (0.1.0-2)

Copies `tprobe2` and its transitive dependencies into the crate and
wraps every `handle_message` dispatch with a probe scope so Goal1
reports per-dispatch latency alongside message count.

- `Cargo.toml`: version bump to 0.1.0-2; add `hdrhistogram = "7"` and
  `minstant = "0.1"` dependencies.
- `src/perf/mod.rs`: new module root; documents vendor source path
  (`../iiac-perf/src`) and divergences (import paths, `// OK: …`
  on `unwrap*`, one diagnostic string).
- `src/perf/tprobe2.rs`: vendored from `iiac-perf/src/tprobe2.rs`.
  Imports rewritten to `crate::perf::{band_table, ticks}`; two
  `unwrap` calls gained `// OK: …` justifications.
- `src/perf/band_table.rs`: vendored from `iiac-perf/src/band_table.rs`.
  Imports rewritten to `crate::perf::fmt` and `crate::perf::ticks`;
  `unwrap_or(0)` calls gained `// OK: …` justifications.
- `src/perf/ticks.rs`: vendored unchanged except for the
  `compile_error!` diagnostic now mentions `actor-x1 probes`.
- `src/perf/ticks/x86_64.rs`: vendored unchanged.
- `src/perf/fmt.rs`: extracted `fmt_commas` and `fmt_commas_f64`
  from `iiac-perf/src/harness.rs` (lines 160–188) — avoids vendoring
  the 393-line harness for two helpers. `unwrap_or(0)` gained a
  `// OK: …` justification.
- `src/lib.rs`: adds `pub mod perf;`.
- `src/runtime.rs`: `SingleThreadRuntime::new(probe_name)` now takes
  a name, embeds a `TProbe2`, and wraps each `handle_message` call
  with `probe.start(dst_id as u64)` / `probe.end(id)`. `Default` impl
  dropped (new takes an arg). `probe_mut()` exposes the probe for
  end-of-run reporting. Unit tests pass a probe name.
- `src/bin/goal1.rs`: new arg parser supports `-t` / `--ticks`
  (raw-ticks display); runtime constructed as `"goal1.dispatch"`;
  calls `rt.probe_mut().report(as_ticks)` after the dispatch loop.
  Usage errors now `exit(2)` with a usage line instead of panicking.

### Smoke measurements (release build)

- `goal1 0.5` — 10.56 M messages in 0.5s (~21.1 M msg/s).
  Per-dispatch: mean 9 ns, mean min-p99 9 ns, p99 max 67.5 µs
  (likely an OS interrupt in the tail).
- `goal1 0.5 -t` — 10.57 M messages in 0.5s (~21.1 M msg/s).
  Per-dispatch: mean 35 tk, mean min-p99 34 tk, p99 max 114,687 tk.

Adding the probe cuts throughput roughly in half vs. 0.1.0-1
(~38.7 → ~21.1 M msg/s). The bot thinks the delta is dominated by
two `rdtsc` reads plus `Vec::push` per dispatch (~20 ns of the
~47 ns per-dispatch cost); the 9 ns measured by the probe is the
`handle_message` body alone.

# Chores-01

Discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible markdown.
There is also a [todo.md](todo.md) file and it tracks tasks and in
general there should be a chore section for each task with the why
and how this task will be completed.

See [Chores format](README.md#chores-format)

## Stage 1 runtime — plan marker (0.1.0-0)

Begins the Stage 1 implementation described in [design.md](design.md).
Multi-step ladder (see [Versioning during development](../CLAUDE.md#versioning)):

- `0.1.0-0` — bootstrap Cargo crate + this plan marker (no behavior).
- `0.1.0-1` — Goal1: two actors, single thread, ping-pong an empty
  `Message` for a caller-supplied duration in seconds. Reports
  total messages handled.
- `0.1.0-2` — vendor `tprobe2` (and its transitive deps: `band_table`,
  `ticks`, `fmt_commas*`) from `../iiac-perf/src` into `src/perf/`.
  Wrap per-dispatch `handle_message` calls with `probe.start`/`probe.end`
  using `site_id = actor_id`; print a band-table report at shutdown
  (ns by default, `-t/--ticks` for raw ticks). The bot thinks promoting
  this to a shared crate is worthwhile once Stage 2 is underway, but
  copying in now avoids a cross-repo dependency for the PoC.
- `0.1.0-3` — Goal2: same workload as Goal1 but each actor on its own
  thread, communicating via `std::sync::mpsc` channels. Shutdown by
  dropping senders. Probe instrumented the same way (per-thread
  `TProbe2`, report on join).
- `0.1.0` — final: drop the `-N` suffix, update `notes/todo.md` and
  `notes/README.md`.

### Design decisions recorded here

- **Actor trait signature** deviates from the design sketch by adding
  `&mut self` and a `&mut dyn Context` parameter, where `Context::send(dst_id, msg)`
  is the only way for a handler to emit messages. Required because
  the empty `Message {}` carries no reply-to information and the
  sketched signature `fn handle_message(msg: Message)` has nowhere
  to thread outbound sends through.
- **Two `Context` implementations**: the single-threaded runtime's
  context writes to a `VecDeque`; the multi-threaded runtime's
  context holds `Sender<Message>` per destination. Actors see
  only `&mut dyn Context` and are agnostic to which runtime is
  driving them.
- **x86_64-only for now**: `tprobe2`'s tick counter uses `rdtsc`
  and `iiac-perf/src/ticks.rs` emits a `compile_error!` on other
  arches. Development target is x86_64 so this is acceptable; the
  restriction is documented at the vendored module.

## Goal1: single-thread ping-pong runtime (0.1.0-1)

Implements Goal1 of Stage 1: two actors, one thread, ping-pong an
empty [`Message`] for a caller-supplied duration in seconds, then
report the message count and throughput.

- `src/lib.rs`: `Message` unit struct, `Actor` trait
  (`handle_message(&mut self, &mut dyn Context, Message)`),
  `Context` trait (`send(dst_id: u32, msg: Message)`), and
  `pub mod runtime`.
- `src/runtime.rs`: `SingleThreadRuntime` owns a
  `Vec<Box<dyn Actor>>` and a `VecDeque<(u32, Message)>`; `run_for`
  pops a message, field-split-borrows `actors` and `queue`,
  constructs a private `SingleCtx` wrapping the queue, dispatches,
  counts. Terminates on deadline or queue drain. Unit tests cover
  seed drain, bounded ping-pong count (11 = seed + 5·2), and
  sequential id assignment.
- `src/bin/goal1.rs`: CLI takes one positional `<duration_secs>`
  f64; constructs two `PingPongActor`s (each replies once per
  received message to its peer), seeds one message into actor 0,
  runs, prints `goal1: <count> messages in <secs>s (<M msg/s>)`.

Smoke run on this box: 0.5 s → ~19.3 M messages → ~38.7 M msg/s.

`pub struct Message;` — equivalent externally, instantiates as
`Message` rather than `Message {}`. Matches clippy's preference for
empty types; trivial to convert back if literal match matters.

message.content: let's to p1-10,p10-p20 .. p90-99,p99-max and why are two of the first,last both 10,10?

content.text:
**Why two rows show `10 ns, 10 ns`**: look at the `-t` output — those rows are at **37 tk** and **38 tk**. Those are different histogram buckets, but at ~3.7 GHz both convert to ~10 ns, and `fmt_commas_f64(..., 0)` rounds to 0 decimals → both display as "10". Different data, same rendered ns. Not a bug, just the display resolution being coarser than the underlying tick delta.

The "bands we can see" gap comes from a different cause: the renderer iterates **histogram buckets** (not samples) and assigns each bucket to the band whose range contains the bucket's cumulative mid-rank. When the distribution is a tight spike with just a few huge buckets, most percentile bands end up with zero buckets assigned and get skipped. The `if band_count[i] == 0 { continue; }` then omits the row.

**To always render the bands you listed**, drop the skip-when-empty guard and emit a row per band regardless. Two variants:

| Variant | Bands rendered | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | `p1-p10, p10-p20, …, p90-p99, p99-max` (11) | Matches your listing literally; `min-p1` disappears but samples at mid-rank < 0.01 would have nowhere to go (we'd reassign them to `p1-p10`) |
| **B** | `min-p1, p1-p10, …, p99-max` (12) | Adds `min-p1` as the first row; the bot thinks this is the spirit of your ask and preserves all data bins |

The bot thinks you probably meant **B** (the `..` in your enumeration likely elides the tail, not the head; dropping `min-p1` loses the bottom 1% of samples in the general case), but your enumeration reads as **A** literally. Which do you want?

**Scope note**: this modification lives in `src/perf/band_table.rs`, which we vendored verbatim from `iiac-perf`. This change diverges from upstream — I'll document it in the `## Vendored perf probe …` chores entry as another divergence. Want me to also drop a quick note in `iiac-perf` later, or just let the drift sit until the shared-lib promotion?

message.content: Maybe we need to add an "inner" in collect better data?

lastPrompt: Maybe we need to add an "inner" in collect better data?

content.text:
Yes — an `inner` loop amortizes the probe overhead across N dispatches per scope, and just as usefully, pushes the stored tick values into a range where `hdrhistogram`'s 0.1% relative buckets actually resolve small variations. Right now our samples live at 37–38 tk, where the bucket width is 1 tk and a real ~2 ns jitter has nowhere to go; at `inner=1000` the scope measures ~38,000 tk and a few hundred ticks of jitter naturally spreads across many buckets, which should populate many more percentile bands *without* any change to `band_table.rs`.

**Concrete proposal**

- CLI: `goal1 <duration_secs> [--inner N] [-t|--ticks]` (default `inner=1`, preserves current behavior).
- Runtime: `run_for(duration, inner: u64) -> u64` wraps `inner` dispatches per probe scope; if the queue empties mid-batch, discard the partial scope.
- `TProbe2`: add `end_batch(id, n)` that records `delta / n` so the histogram always stores per-event cost — the report reads per-dispatch no matter what `inner` is. Existing `end(id)` becomes `end_batch(id, 1)` semantically.

**Tradeoff**: you lose per-dispatch outliers — a single OS preemption inside a batch shows up as `1/inner` of its true magnitude smeared across the scope. For a ping-pong workload that's an acceptable loss since we're measuring average dispatch cost anyway, but if we later want to catch individual tail events we'd want a second probe with inner=1 (or a separate mode flag).

**Recommendation**: do `inner` first, then re-examine the band-rendering output. The bot thinks the "two 10/10 rows" and "only 4 bands" issues will both resolve on their own once the histogram has real dynamic range to work with. If they don't, we can still switch to always-show-all-bands after.

Sound good? And do you want `inner` as its own step (`0.1.0-3`) and push Goal2 to `-4`, or fold it into the end of `0.1.0-2`?

message.content: We also should add a --warmup with a default of 10s, maybe time to move to clap?

content.text:
Yes, time for clap. We're at four args (`<duration>`, `--inner`, `--warmup`, `-t/--ticks`) with defaults and validation — the hand-rolled parser has outgrown its complexity budget, and iiac-perf already uses `clap = { version = "4", features = ["derive", "wrap_help"] }` so we have a shape to match.

**Warmup semantics** (proposing):
- Run the dispatch loop identically for `--warmup <seconds>` (default **10s**).
- Clear the probe (new `TProbe2::clear()` that empties both records and the histogram).
- Run the measurement loop for `<duration_secs>`, record, report.
- **Only measurement-phase counts** are printed — warmup is silent.

`TProbe2::clear()` is a small addition (`self.records.clear(); self.hist.clear();` — hdrhistogram has `.clear()`) and genuinely belongs on the type. Divergence from upstream noted in chores.

**Revised step 0.1.0-3** (all as one commit):
- Add `clap` dep to `Cargo.toml`.
- `Cli` derive-struct in `src/bin/goal1.rs` with `duration_s` positional, `--inner` (default 1), `--warmup` (default 10.0), `-t/--ticks`.
- Add `TProbe2::clear()` + `TProbe2::end_batch(id, n)`.
- `SingleThreadRuntime::run_for(duration, inner)` — scope wraps `inner` dispatches; partial trailing batch discarded (no record, no count).
- New `SingleThreadRuntime::warmup_for(duration, inner)` — same loop, same record/count accounting, but caller clears the probe afterward.

**Revised ladder**: `0.1.0-3` = inner + warmup + clap (this step), `0.1.0-4` = Goal2, `0.1.0` = final. Five total commits, vs. four planned — one extra but they're each small.

**Tradeoffs worth naming**:
- clap pulls in ~15 small crates (proc-macro2, syn, quote, …) — compile time goes up ~3–5s first build. Not runtime cost.
- "Partial trailing batch discarded" means very short `duration` may record 0 samples. At `--inner 1000` and duration 0.001s that's likely; at typical usage (duration ≥ 0.1s, inner ≤ 10000) it's a non-issue.
- Warmup messages still count as messages handled from the system's perspective, but we don't expose that count. The bot thinks that's the right call — users care about measured throughput, not total work done.

OK to proceed with step 0.1.0-3 as specified? Any tweaks to warmup default or semantics before I start?

message.content: From a timing pov warmup shouldn't count, I don't think at a minimum it introduces more noise making it harder to evaluate code changes, thoughts?

lastPrompt: From a timing pov warmup shouldn't count, I don't think at a minimum it introduces more noise making it harder to evaluate code changes, thoughts?

content.text:
Agreed — as proposed, the warmup count is already discarded (the `u64` from `warmup_for` goes nowhere; only `run_for`'s count drives the throughput line). Your reasoning is the right framing: each reported number reflects exactly `<duration>` seconds of stable-state dispatch, so code-change A/B is clean.

One small design choice worth settling while we're here — does warmup run the probe or not?

- **Option X**: warmup calls `probe.start`/`end` same as measurement; we clear records after. Upside: warmup's instruction stream is identical to measurement's, so cache/branch predictor/freq land in the same state. Only difference between phases is `probe.clear()` at the boundary.
- **Option Y**: warmup skips the probe calls entirely. Slightly fewer instructions during warmup; no records to clear.

The bot thinks **X** is the more defensible default — the whole point of warmup is to match what measurement will look like, and the probe itself is part of the measurement-phase hot path. Also collapses down to one shared inner helper instead of two divergent loops. Sound right?

message.content: X, also eventually I want to subtract measure & loop overhead which we'd do after warmup, thoughts?

content.text:
Good call, and "after warmup" is the right point in the pipeline — calibration needs a thermally-stable system to produce meaningful numbers.

**Two different things worth disambiguating**:
- **Measure overhead** (what iiac-perf's `overhead.rs` subtracts) — the cost *inside* the scope that isn't the measured work: the second `rdtsc` return, the call/ret into `end()`, and any out-of-order serialization. Calibrated by running many empty `probe.start(); probe.end()` pairs with no work between and taking the min/p1 of the delta. This is what contaminates probe readings today.
- **Loop overhead** — the per-iteration `pop_front`, deadline check, split-borrow, `SingleCtx` construction. This is *outside* the probe scope in our current design, so it doesn't pollute probe readings — but it does account for a chunk of the gap between per-dispatch probe mean (~9 ns) and total throughput (~47 ns/dispatch). The bot thinks this one doesn't need subtraction from probe samples; it'd be a separate diagnostic line ("loop overhead ≈ total_ns/count − probe_mean") if we want it visible.

**Recommended step split**:
- `0.1.0-3` — inner + warmup + clap (Option X). Keep it scoped.
- `0.1.0-4` — overhead calibration: add `Overhead` struct (vendored-ish from iiac-perf), calibration phase between warmup and measurement, subtract from probe samples at report time. `--raw` or `--no-overhead` flag added then, not preemptively.
- `0.1.0-5` — Goal2.
- `0.1.0` — final.

Six commits now, which is more than we started with, but each is focused. Alternative if six feels too many: fold overhead into `0.1.0-4` *alongside* Goal2 — risks a noisier diff but saves one commit. The bot leans toward keeping them separate since overhead calibration touches the perf module and Goal2 touches the runtime module — different surface areas.

Start `0.1.0-3` now?

Adding the probe cuts throughput roughly in half vs. 0.1.0-1
(~38.7 → ~21.1 M msg/s). The bot thinks the delta is dominated by
two `rdtsc` reads plus `Vec::push` per dispatch (~20 ns of the
~47 ns per-dispatch cost); the 9 ns measured by the probe is the
`handle_message` body alone.

## Inner batching, warmup, clap (0.1.0-3)

Adds CLI polish and measurement-quality improvements to Goal1.
Scope:

- **Inner batching** — one probe scope now covers `--inner N`
  consecutive dispatches (default 1). At larger `N` the probe's
  two-`rdtsc`-plus-push apparatus cost is amortized, and stored
  tick values land in a range where the histogram's 0.1 %-relative
  buckets actually resolve jitter.
- **Warmup** — `--warmup SECS` (default **10.0**) runs the same
  dispatch loop with the probe active, then clears probe state;
  measurement phase inherits stable-state CPU freq / cache /
  branch-predictor conditions. Warmup message counts are never
  reported.
- **clap** — hand-rolled arg parsing replaced with a derive-based
  `Cli`. Validates non-negative finite `duration_s` and `warmup`;
  enforces `inner ≥ 1`. Auto-generated `--help` / `--version`.
- **TProbe2 additions** (vendored-code divergence from iiac-perf):
  - `Record` gains a `batch: u64` field.
  - `TProbe2::end_batch(id, n)` appends with `batch = n`; existing
    `end(id)` sets `batch = 1`.
  - `TProbe2::report` now divides stored deltas by `batch` at
    drain time so the histogram always holds per-event cost,
    regardless of scope granularity.
  - `TProbe2::clear()` resets records buffer and histogram;
    called between warmup and measurement.

File-level edits:

- `Cargo.toml`: bump to 0.1.0-3; add
  `clap = { version = "4", features = ["derive", "wrap_help"] }`.
- `src/perf/tprobe2.rs`: `Record.batch`, `end_batch`, `clear`,
  per-event division in `report`. Doc comment at file head lists
  divergences from upstream. Three new tests
  (`end_batch_stores_batch_size`, `report_divides_batched_delta`,
  `clear_empties_records_and_hist`).
- `src/runtime.rs`: `run_for(duration, inner)` wraps each batch
  of `inner` dispatches in a probe scope; site_id is the first
  `dst_id` in the batch. Partial trailing batches (queue empties
  mid-scope) are discarded — scope dropped, count not incremented
  — so throughput numbers reflect whole-batch work only. New test
  `partial_trailing_batch_is_dropped` covers the drop path.
- `src/bin/goal1.rs`: clap-derive `Cli` with `duration_s`,
  `--warmup`, `--inner`, `-t/--ticks`. Runs warmup, clears probe,
  runs measurement, prints count/throughput (now also reports
  `inner=N`), then calls `probe.report`.

### Smoke measurements (release, `--warmup 0`)

| Args | Throughput | Probe mean (min-p99) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `0.5` (inner=1) | 19.7 M msg/s | 9 ns | Apparatus overhead dominates, distribution stuck in two spike buckets |
| `0.5 --inner 1000` | 205 M msg/s | 5 ns | ~10× throughput; per-dispatch mean drops because apparatus is amortized |
| `0.5 --inner 1000 -t` | 204 M msg/s | 18 tk | Ticks view; tail p99-max 449 tk vs ~115 k tk at inner=1 (batch smooths OS interrupts) |

Warmup wall-clock sanity: `goal1 0.3 --warmup 1 --inner 1000` ran
in 1.33s total. Argv error paths (`-1`, `--inner 0`, `--warmup nan`)
all exit 2 with clap-formatted messages.

The bot thinks the inner=1 vs inner=1000 gap (9 ns vs 5 ns
per-event) is ~4 ns of apparatus overhead; 0.1.0-4's overhead
calibration will confirm that estimate directly.

  - `TProbe2::end_batch(id, n)` appends with `batch = n`; existing
    `end(id)` sets `batch = 1`.
  - `TProbe2::report` now divides stored deltas by `batch` at
    drain time so the histogram always holds per-event cost,
    regardless of scope granularity.
  - `TProbe2::clear()` resets records buffer and histogram;
    called between warmup and measurement.

File-level edits:

- `Cargo.toml`: bump to 0.1.0-3; add
  `clap = { version = "4", features = ["derive", "wrap_help"] }`.
- `src/perf/tprobe2.rs`: `Record.batch`, `end_batch`, `clear`,
  per-event division in `report`. Doc comment at file head lists
  divergences from upstream. Three new tests
  (`end_batch_stores_batch_size`, `report_divides_batched_delta`,
  `clear_empties_records_and_hist`).
- `src/runtime.rs`: `run_for(duration, inner)` wraps each batch
  of `inner` dispatches in a probe scope; site_id is the first
  `dst_id` in the batch. Partial trailing batches (queue empties
  mid-scope) are discarded — scope dropped, count not incremented
  — so throughput numbers reflect whole-batch work only. New test
  `partial_trailing_batch_is_dropped` covers the drop path.
- `src/bin/goal1.rs`: clap-derive `Cli` with `duration_s`,
  `--warmup`, `--inner`, `-t/--ticks`. Runs warmup, clears probe,
  runs measurement, prints count/throughput (now also reports
  `inner=N`), then calls `probe.report`.

### Smoke measurements (release, `--warmup 0`)

| Args | Throughput | Probe mean (min-p99) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `0.5` (inner=1) | 19.7 M msg/s | 9 ns | Apparatus overhead dominates, distribution stuck in two spike buckets |
| `0.5 --inner 1000` | 205 M msg/s | 5 ns | ~10× throughput; per-dispatch mean drops because apparatus is amortized |
| `0.5 --inner 1000 -t` | 204 M msg/s | 18 tk | Ticks view; tail p99-max 449 tk vs ~115 k tk at inner=1 (batch smooths OS interrupts) |

Warmup wall-clock sanity: `goal1 0.3 --warmup 1 --inner 1000` ran
in 1.33s total. Argv error paths (`-1`, `--inner 0`, `--warmup nan`)
all exit 2 with clap-formatted messages.

The bot thinks the inner=1 vs inner=1000 gap (9 ns vs 5 ns
per-event) is ~4 ns of apparatus overhead; 0.1.0-4's overhead
calibration will confirm that estimate directly.

# Chores-01

Discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible markdown.
There is also a [todo.md](todo.md) file and it tracks tasks and in
general there should be a chore section for each task with the why
and how this task will be completed.

See [Chores format](README.md#chores-format)

## Stage 1 runtime — plan marker (0.1.0-0)

Begins the Stage 1 implementation described in [design.md](design.md).
Multi-step ladder (see [Versioning during development](../CLAUDE.md#versioning)):

- `0.1.0-0` — bootstrap Cargo crate + this plan marker (no behavior).
- `0.1.0-1` — Goal1: two actors, single thread, ping-pong an empty
  `Message` for a caller-supplied duration in seconds. Reports
  total messages handled.
- `0.1.0-2` — vendor `tprobe2` (and its transitive deps: `band_table`,
  `ticks`, `fmt_commas*`) from `../iiac-perf/src` into `src/perf/`.
  Wrap per-dispatch `handle_message` calls with `probe.start`/`probe.end`
  using `site_id = actor_id`; print a band-table report at shutdown
  (ns by default, `-t/--ticks` for raw ticks). The bot thinks promoting
  this to a shared crate is worthwhile once Stage 2 is underway, but
  copying in now avoids a cross-repo dependency for the PoC.
- `0.1.0-3` — Goal2: same workload as Goal1 but each actor on its own
  thread, communicating via `std::sync::mpsc` channels. Shutdown by
  dropping senders. Probe instrumented the same way (per-thread
  `TProbe2`, report on join).
- `0.1.0` — final: drop the `-N` suffix, update `notes/todo.md` and
  `notes/README.md`.

### Design decisions recorded here

- **Actor trait signature** deviates from the design sketch by adding
  `&mut self` and a `&mut dyn Context` parameter, where `Context::send(dst_id, msg)`
  is the only way for a handler to emit messages. Required because
  the empty `Message {}` carries no reply-to information and the
  sketched signature `fn handle_message(msg: Message)` has nowhere
  to thread outbound sends through.
- **Two `Context` implementations**: the single-threaded runtime's
  context writes to a `VecDeque`; the multi-threaded runtime's
  context holds `Sender<Message>` per destination. Actors see
  only `&mut dyn Context` and are agnostic to which runtime is
  driving them.
- **x86_64-only for now**: `tprobe2`'s tick counter uses `rdtsc`
  and `iiac-perf/src/ticks.rs` emits a `compile_error!` on other
  arches. Development target is x86_64 so this is acceptable; the
  restriction is documented at the vendored module.

## Goal1: single-thread ping-pong runtime (0.1.0-1)

Implements Goal1 of Stage 1: two actors, one thread, ping-pong an
empty [`Message`] for a caller-supplied duration in seconds, then
report the message count and throughput.

- `src/lib.rs`: `Message` unit struct, `Actor` trait
  (`handle_message(&mut self, &mut dyn Context, Message)`),
  `Context` trait (`send(dst_id: u32, msg: Message)`), and
  `pub mod runtime`.
- `src/runtime.rs`: `SingleThreadRuntime` owns a
  `Vec<Box<dyn Actor>>` and a `VecDeque<(u32, Message)>`; `run_for`
  pops a message, field-split-borrows `actors` and `queue`,
  constructs a private `SingleCtx` wrapping the queue, dispatches,
  counts. Terminates on deadline or queue drain. Unit tests cover
  seed drain, bounded ping-pong count (11 = seed + 5·2), and
  sequential id assignment.
- `src/bin/goal1.rs`: CLI takes one positional `<duration_secs>`
  f64; constructs two `PingPongActor`s (each replies once per
  received message to its peer), seeds one message into actor 0,
  runs, prints `goal1: <count> messages in <secs>s (<M msg/s>)`.

Smoke run on this box: 0.5 s → ~19.3 M messages → ~38.7 M msg/s.

### Deviation from the design sketch

`struct Message {}` in `design.md` is realised as a unit struct
`pub struct Message;` — equivalent externally, instantiates as
`Message` rather than `Message {}`. Matches clippy's preference for
empty types; trivial to convert back if literal match matters.

## Vendored perf probe + Goal1 instrumentation (0.1.0-2)

Copies `tprobe2` and its transitive dependencies into the crate and
wraps every `handle_message` dispatch with a probe scope so Goal1
reports per-dispatch latency alongside message count.

- `Cargo.toml`: version bump to 0.1.0-2; add `hdrhistogram = "7"` and
  `minstant = "0.1"` dependencies.
- `src/perf/mod.rs`: new module root; documents vendor source path
  (`../iiac-perf/src`) and divergences (import paths, `// OK: …`
  on `unwrap*`, one diagnostic string).
- `src/perf/tprobe2.rs`: vendored from `iiac-perf/src/tprobe2.rs`.
  Imports rewritten to `crate::perf::{band_table, ticks}`; two
  `unwrap` calls gained `// OK: …` justifications.
- `src/perf/band_table.rs`: vendored from `iiac-perf/src/band_table.rs`.
  Imports rewritten to `crate::perf::fmt` and `crate::perf::ticks`;
  `unwrap_or(0)` calls gained `// OK: …` justifications.
- `src/perf/ticks.rs`: vendored unchanged except for the
  `compile_error!` diagnostic now mentions `actor-x1 probes`.
- `src/perf/ticks/x86_64.rs`: vendored unchanged.
- `src/perf/fmt.rs`: extracted `fmt_commas` and `fmt_commas_f64`
  from `iiac-perf/src/harness.rs` (lines 160–188) — avoids vendoring
  the 393-line harness for two helpers. `unwrap_or(0)` gained a
  `// OK: …` justification.
- `src/lib.rs`: adds `pub mod perf;`.
- `src/runtime.rs`: `SingleThreadRuntime::new(probe_name)` now takes
  a name, embeds a `TProbe2`, and wraps each `handle_message` call
  with `probe.start(dst_id as u64)` / `probe.end(id)`. `Default` impl
  dropped (new takes an arg). `probe_mut()` exposes the probe for
  end-of-run reporting. Unit tests pass a probe name.
- `src/bin/goal1.rs`: new arg parser supports `-t` / `--ticks`
  (raw-ticks display); runtime constructed as `"goal1.dispatch"`;
  calls `rt.probe_mut().report(as_ticks)` after the dispatch loop.
  Usage errors now `exit(2)` with a usage line instead of panicking.

### Smoke measurements (release build)

- `goal1 0.5` — 10.56 M messages in 0.5s (~21.1 M msg/s).
  Per-dispatch: mean 9 ns, mean min-p99 9 ns, p99 max 67.5 µs
  (likely an OS interrupt in the tail).
- `goal1 0.5 -t` — 10.57 M messages in 0.5s (~21.1 M msg/s).
  Per-dispatch: mean 35 tk, mean min-p99 34 tk, p99 max 114,687 tk.

Adding the probe cuts throughput roughly in half vs. 0.1.0-1

two `rdtsc` reads plus `Vec::push` per dispatch (~20 ns of the
~47 ns per-dispatch cost); the 9 ns measured by the probe is the
`handle_message` body alone.

Stop — I need to flag a mistake I made before we commit. I skipped committing step 0.1.0-2 entirely. After "Ready for your review" on 0.1.0-2, I went straight into the band-display discussion, then inner/warmup/clap, and implemented 0.1.0-3 on top of the still-uncommitted 0.1.0-2 working copy. `jj log` confirms: `@-` is 0.1.0-1, and the working copy contains both 0.1.0-2 and 0.1.0-3 changes combined.

Three ways out:

1. **Single commit at 0.1.0-3** — commit everything as-is; chores has both `## (0.1.0-2)` and `## (0.1.0-3)` sections inside one commit labeled 0.1.0-3. Simple, but the history mentions a 0.1.0-2 that never existed as its own commit.
2. **Single commit at 0.1.0-2** — renumber Cargo.toml back to `0.1.0-2`, merge the two chores sections into one, drop the ladder step. Clean one-to-one between commit version and Cargo version, but we lose the `-2 → -3` split we planned.
3. **Split via `jj split`** — interactively split the working copy: revert 0.1.0-3 changes to files (tprobe2.rs, runtime.rs, goal1.rs, Cargo.toml, chores-01.md), commit as 0.1.0-2, then re-apply 0.1.0-3 and commit. Clean history, more work — fiddly because the split cuts through the middle of several files.

The bot thinks **1** is the pragmatic call: the two-phase conceptual split stays visible in chores (future readers see exactly what thinking happened), and no information is lost. **2** is a close runner-up if you want version = commit parity. **3** is the "right" answer for history purity but costs us time on a PoC.

Your call — which do you want?
